title_wylie: shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa'i snying po
title_english: The Heart of the Transcendent Perfection of Wisdom
source: IOL Tib J 751, India Office Library (now British Library), London
date_evidence: Dated by colophon reference to the Tibetan imperial period. The manuscript belongs to the period of Tibetan control of Dunhuang (786–848 CE).
notes: One of the earliest known Tibetan manuscripts of the Heart Sūtra. Short recension without frame narrative. Preserves early Tibetan translation conventions from the imperial period. Lacks the prologue and epilogue found in Kangyur long recension (Toh 21). Important for establishing the short recension's early circulation in Tibetan.
significance: Earliest securely datable Tibetan Heart Sūtra witness
Short recension — corresponds to T251 (Chinese) and Hōryūji manuscript (Sanskrit)
Pre-dates all printed Kangyur editions by several centuries
Imperial-period orthography may preserve older translation conventions
Confirms short recension circulated independently in Tibetan
relationship_to_toh21: Toh 21 is the long recension with frame narrative. IOL Tib J 751 is the short recension without frame narrative. The core text (Avalokiteśvara's teaching) overlaps, but differs in orthographic details reflecting earlier translation conventions.

segments.wylie: byang chub sems dpa' sems dpa' chen po 'phags pa spyan ras gzigs dbang phyug shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa zab mo'i spyod pa nyid la rnam par blta zhing / phung po lnga po de dag la yang rang bzhin gyis stong par rnam par blta'o/
segments.english_gloss: The bodhisattva mahāsattva noble Avalokiteśvara was looking upon the profound practice of prajñāpāramitā, and he saw that the five aggregates are empty of inherent nature.
segments.note: Short recension omits frame narrative ('at that time' temporal marker found in Toh 21)

segments.tibetan: གཟུགས་སྟོང་པའོ། སྟོང་པ་ཉིད་གཟུགས་སོ། གཟུགས་ལས་སྟོང་པ་ཉིད་གཞན་མ་ཡིན། སྟོང་པ་ཉིད་ལས་ཀྱང་གཟུགས་གཞན་མ་ཡིན་ནོ། དེ་བཞིན་དུ་ཚོར་བ་དང་། འདུ་ཤེས་དང་། འདུ་བྱེད་དང་། རྣམ་པར་ཤེས་པ་རྣམས་སྟོང་པའོ།
segments.wylie: gzugs stong pa'o/ stong pa nyid gzugs so/ gzugs las stong pa nyid gzhan ma yin/ stong pa nyid las kyang gzugs gzhan ma yin no/ de bzhin du tshor ba dang / 'du shes dang / 'du byed dang / rnam par shes pa rnams stong pa'o/
segments.english_gloss: Form is emptiness; emptiness is form. Emptiness is not other than form; form is not other than emptiness. Likewise, feeling, perception, formation, and consciousness are empty.

segments.tibetan: ཤཱ་རིའི་བུ། དེ་ལྟར་ཆོས་ཐམས་ཅད་སྟོང་པ་ཉིད་དེ། མཚན་ཉིད་མེད་པ། མ་སྐྱེས་པ། མ་འགགས་པ། དྲི་མ་མེད་པ། དྲི་མ་དང་བྲལ་བ་མེད་པ། བྲི་བ་མེད་པ། གང་བ་མེད་པའོ།
segments.wylie: shA ri'i bu/ de ltar chos thams cad stong pa nyid de/ mtshan nyid med pa/ ma skyes pa/ ma 'gags pa/ dri ma med pa/ dri ma dang bral ba med pa/ bri ba med pa/ gang ba med pa'o/
segments.english_gloss: Śāriputra, thus all dharmas are emptiness: without characteristics, not arising, not ceasing, not defiled, not undefiled, not decreasing, not increasing.

segments.tibetan: ཤཱ་རིའི་བུ། དེ་ལྟ་བས་ན་སྟོང་པ་ཉིད་ལ་གཟུགས་མེད། ཚོར་བ་མེད། འདུ་ཤེས་མེད། འདུ་བྱེད་རྣམས་མེད། རྣམ་པར་ཤེས་པ་མེད།
segments.wylie: shA ri'i bu/ de lta bas na stong pa nyid la gzugs med/ tshor ba med/ 'du shes med/ 'du byed rnams med/ rnam par shes pa med/
segments.english_gloss: Śāriputra, therefore in emptiness there is no form, no feeling, no perception, no formations, no consciousness;

segments.tibetan: མིག་མེད། རྣ་བ་མེད། སྣ་མེད། ལྕེ་མེད། ལུས་མེད། ཡིད་མེད། གཟུགས་མེད། སྒྲ་མེད། དྲི་མེད། རོ་མེད། རེག་བྱ་མེད། ཆོས་མེད་དོ།
segments.wylie: mig med/ rna ba med/ sna med/ lce med/ lus med/ yid med/ gzugs med/ sgra med/ dri med/ ro med/ reg bya med/ chos med do/
segments.english_gloss: no eye, no ear, no nose, no tongue, no body, no mind; no form, no sound, no smell, no taste, no touch, no mental objects.

segments.wylie: mig gi khams med pa nas yid kyi khams med/ yid kyi rnam par shes pa'i khams kyi bar du yang med do/
segments.english_gloss: No eye-element up to no mind-element; no mind-consciousness element either.

segments.wylie: ma rig pa med/ ma rig pa zad pa med pa nas/ rga shi med/ rga shi zad pa'i bar du yang med do/
segments.english_gloss: No ignorance, no ending of ignorance, up to no old age and death, no ending of old age and death.
segments.note: Uses zad pa (exhaustion/kṣaya) matching Kangyur and Sanskrit; supports back-translation thesis

segments.tibetan: སྡུག་བསྔལ་བ་དང་། ཀུན་འབྱུང་བ་དང་། འགོག་པ་དང་། ལམ་མེད། ཡེ་ཤེས་མེད། ཐོབ་པ་མེད། མ་ཐོབ་པ་ཡང་མེད་དོ།
segments.wylie: sdug bsngal ba dang / kun 'byung ba dang / 'gog pa dang / lam med/ ye shes med/ thob pa med/ ma thob pa yang med do/
segments.english_gloss: No suffering, no origination, no cessation, no path; no wisdom, no attainment, and no non-attainment.

segments.wylie: shA ri'i bu/ de lta bas na byang chub sems dpa' rnams thob pa med pa'i phyir/ shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa la brten cing gnas te/ sems la sgrib pa med pas 'jigs pa med de/ phyin ci log las shin tu 'das nas mya ngan las 'das pa'i mthar phyin to/
segments.english_gloss: Śāriputra, therefore, because there is no attainment, bodhisattvas rely on and abide in prajñāpāramitā. Because their minds have no obscurations, they have no fear. Having completely transcended error, they attain the final nirvāṇa.

segments.wylie: dus gsum du rnam par bzhugs pa'i sangs rgyas thams cad kyang shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa la brten nas bla na med pa yang dag par rdzogs pa'i byang chub tu mngon par rdzogs par sangs rgyas so/
segments.english_gloss: All the buddhas who abide in the three times, relying on prajñāpāramitā, fully awaken to unsurpassed, perfect, complete enlightenment.

segments.wylie: de lta bas na shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa'i sngags/ rig pa chen po'i sngags/ bla na med pa'i sngags/ mi mnyam pa dang mnyam pa'i sngags/ sdug bsngal thams cad rab tu zhi bar byed pa'i sngags/ mi rdzun pas na bden par shes par bya ste/ shes rab kyi pha rol tu phyin pa'i sngags smras pa/
segments.english_gloss: Therefore, the prajñāpāramitā mantra, the mantra of great knowledge, the unsurpassed mantra, the mantra equal to the unequalled, the mantra that completely pacifies all suffering, should be known as true because it is not false. The prajñāpāramitā mantra is proclaimed:
segments.note: Five epithets matching Kangyur tradition, vs. four in T251 and three in T250

segments.wylie: tad+ya thA/ ga te ga te pA ra ga te pA ra saM ga te bo d+hi swA hA/
segments.english_gloss: Tadyathā: gate gate pāragate pārasaṃgate bodhi svāhā.
segments.note: Short recension manuscript lacks oṃ before mantra, unlike Kangyur Toh 21 which has ཨོཾ. This matches T251 and the earliest Sanskrit witnesses.

scholarly_refs: De La Vallée Poussin, Louis (1962). Catalogue of the Tibetan Manuscripts from Tun-huang in the India Office Library. Oxford.
Silk, Jonathan A. (1994). The Heart Sūtra in Tibetan: A Critical Edition of the Two Recensions Contained in the Kanjur. Vienna.
Dalton, Jacob & van Schaik, Sam (2006). Tibetan Tantric Manuscripts from Dunhuang. Leiden: Brill.

idp.name: International Dunhuang Project
idp.note: Digital images of IOL Tib J 751 available through the IDP database
